title: Mathematics
subtitle: Week 3: [Advanced] Parallel, Intersecting, and Perpendicular Lines

slides.title: Lines in Our Environment
slides.text: Let's explore how lines relate to each other in space. Lines can run side-by-side, cross each other, or meet at special angles to form shapes. In math, we express this as: Line relationships = shapes and positions. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Parallel lines are lines on a flat surface that always stay the same distance apart and never cross. Intersecting lines are lines that cross each other at a single shared point called the intersection. Perpendicular lines are a special type of intersecting lines that cross to form a right angle. A right angle is exactly ninety degrees, which looks like the square corner of a book or sheet of paper. Can you look around the room and find where two lines meet?

examples.title: Example 1
examples.content: Review standard details: Line relationships = shapes and positions.

slides.title: What are Parallel Lines?
slides.text: Parallel lines are straight lines on a flat surface that always stay the same distance apart. No matter how far they extend, they will never cross. In math, we express this as: Parallel lines = never meet, constant distance. . . . . . Can you draw two parallel lines on your paper using a ruler?

examples.title: Example 2
examples.content: Review standard details: Parallel lines = never meet, constant distance.

slides.title: Parallel Lines in Daily Life
slides.text: We see models of parallel lines everywhere. The rails of a train track, the opposite edges of a ruler, or parallel crop rows in Polomolok farms. In math, we express this as: Crop rows = parallel paths in farms. . . . . . Why must train tracks be parallel to each other to be safe?

examples.title: Example 3
examples.content: Review standard details: Crop rows = parallel paths in farms.

slides.title: Recognizing Parallel Lines
slides.text: We can test if two lines are parallel by measuring the perpendicular distance between them at different points. The distance must be exactly the same everywhere. In math, we express this as: Distance d1 = d2 at all points. . . . . . If the distance between two paths changes, are they still parallel?

examples.title: Example 4
examples.content: Review standard details: Distance d1 = d2 at all points.

slides.title: What are Intersecting Lines?
slides.text: Intersecting lines are straight lines that cross or meet each other at a single shared point. We call this crossing point the point of intersection. In math, we express this as: Intersecting lines = cross at a single point. . . . . . Can you find where two streets cross to make an intersection?

examples.title: Example 5
examples.content: Review standard details: Intersecting lines = cross at a single point.

slides.title: Finding Intersecting Lines
slides.text: When two lines cross, they form an X-like shape. The point where they meet is a point that lies on both lines at the same time. In math, we express this as: Intersection = shared crossing dot. . . . . . How many points do two straight intersecting lines share with each other?

examples.title: Example 6
examples.content: Review standard details: Intersection = shared crossing dot.

slides.title: What are Perpendicular Lines?
slides.text: Perpendicular lines are a special type of intersecting lines. When they cross, they form four equal right angles that look like square corners. In math, we express this as: Perpendicular lines = cross at a right angle. . . . . . What symbol do we use to show that lines are perpendicular?

examples.title: Example 7
examples.content: Review standard details: Perpendicular lines = cross at a right angle.

slides.title: Understanding the Right Angle
slides.text: A right angle is an angle of exactly ninety degrees. It forms a perfect L-shape, like the corner of your classroom blackboard or book cover. In math, we express this as: Right angle = exactly 90 degrees. . . . . . How can you use a piece of paper to check if an angle is right?

examples.title: Example 8
examples.content: Review standard details: Right angle = exactly 90 degrees.

slides.title: Perpendicular Lines in Real Life
slides.text: We see perpendicular lines in many places. Street crossings in Koronadal City, window frames, or the corner joints of a wooden pineapple crate are perpendicular. In math, we express this as: Street corner = 90 degree crossing. . . . . . Why are building walls built perpendicular to the flat floor surface?

examples.title: Example 9
examples.content: Review standard details: Street corner = 90 degree crossing.

slides.title: Comparing Line Relationships
slides.text: Let's compare: parallel lines never meet. Intersecting lines meet at any angle. Perpendicular lines meet at exactly a ninety-degree right angle in a corner. In math, we express this as: Parallel vs Intersecting vs Perpendicular. . . . . . We use a standard ruler and a square corner template to draw perpendicular lines accurately. Which type of lines form the corner of a square tile?

examples.title: Example 10
examples.content: Review standard details: Parallel vs Intersecting vs Perpendicular.

slides.title: Drawing Parallel Lines
slides.text: To draw parallel lines, we place our ruler flat, draw one line along the top edge, slide the ruler down, and draw the second line. In math, we express this as: Step-by-step ruler guide. . . . . . . How does a ruler help us keep the distance between lines equal?

examples.title: Example 11
examples.content: Review standard details: Step-by-step ruler guide.

slides.title: Drawing Perpendicular Lines
slides.text: To draw perpendicular lines, we draw a flat horizontal line first, then use a square template or protractor to draw a vertical line straight up. In math, we express this as: Drawing square corner angles. . . . . . . Why is a square corner template helpful when drawing perpendicular paths?

examples.title: Example 12
examples.content: Review standard details: Drawing square corner angles.

slides.title: Drawing Intersecting Lines
slides.text: Drawing intersecting lines is simple. We draw two straight lines that cross each other at any angle, and mark the crossing point with a dot. In math, we express this as: Creating cross line patterns. . . . . . Can you label the intersection point of your lines as Point P?

examples.title: Example 13
examples.content: Review standard details: Creating cross line patterns.

slides.title: Drawing Equal Line Segments
slides.text: Let's draw two different line segments of equal length. We use a ruler to draw Segment AB as 6 cm, and Segment CD as 6 cm. In math, we express this as: Length AB = Length CD = 6 cm. . . . . . Does the direction of the segment change its measured length value?

examples.title: Example 14
examples.content: Review standard details: Length AB = Length CD = 6 cm.

slides.title: Measuring Equal Segments
slides.text: We can verify that two segments are equal by measuring both with a metric ruler. If both measure fifty millimeters, they are equal in length. In math, we express this as: AB = CD = 50 mm. . . . . . Why is accurate measurement important when cutting wood to build frames?

examples.title: Example 15
examples.content: Review standard details: AB = CD = 50 mm.

slides.title: Parallel Weaving in Lake Sebu
slides.text: Weavers in Lake Sebu use parallel stripes in their patterns. They align colorful threads side-by-side to create symmetric borders on their T'nalak cloth. In math, we express this as: Parallel stripes = traditional T'nalak design. . . . . . . How do parallel lines make hand-woven patterns look neat and organized?

examples.title: Example 16
examples.content: Review standard details: Parallel stripes = traditional T'nalak design.

slides.title: Grid Layouts in Communities
slides.text: Town planners in Tupi design community grids using parallel and perpendicular streets. This makes it easy to organize blocks of houses and parks. In math, we express this as: Street grids = parallel and perpendicular blocks. . . . . . How does a grid layout help people navigate a town easily?

examples.title: Example 17
examples.content: Review standard details: Street grids = parallel and perpendicular blocks.

slides.title: Finding Corners in Shapes
slides.text: Rectangles and squares are made of perpendicular lines. Every corner of a rectangle is a right angle where two perpendicular sides meet each other. In math, we express this as: Square corners = perpendicular sides of rectangles. . . . . . How many right angles can you find in a standard square?

examples.title: Example 18
examples.content: Review standard details: Square corners = perpendicular sides of rectangles.

slides.title: Line Types in Alphabet Letters
slides.text: Look at alphabet letters: H has parallel and perpendicular lines. X has intersecting lines. T has perpendicular lines. E has parallel and perpendicular lines. In math, we express this as: Letter lines = parallel, intersecting, perpendicular. . . . . . Can you find a letter in your name that has parallel lines?

examples.title: Example 19
examples.content: Review standard details: Letter lines = parallel, intersecting, perpendicular.

slides.title: Mount Matutum Trail Maps
slides.text: On a map of Mount Matutum trails, hiking paths represent lines. Some trails run parallel to each other, while others intersect at camp locations. In math, we express this as: Trail crossing = intersecting paths. . . . . . . What does an intersection of two trails represent for hikers?

examples.title: Example 20
examples.content: Review standard details: Trail crossing = intersecting paths.

slides.title: Building Wooden Crates
slides.text: Farmers build wooden crates for pineapples using perpendicular joints. Connecting the wooden boards at ninety-degree right angles makes the crates strong and stable. In math, we express this as: Crate joints = perpendicular corner connections. . . . . . . What would happen if the crate corners were not perpendicular joints?

examples.title: Example 21
examples.content: Review standard details: Crate joints = perpendicular corner connections.

slides.title: Common Error: Non-Parallel Lines
slides.text: A common error is drawing two lines that look parallel but are slightly tilted. If extended far enough, these lines will eventually intersect each other. In math, we express this as: Non-parallel lines = eventually cross in distance. . . . . . Why must we measure the distance at both ends to confirm parallel lines?

examples.title: Example 22
examples.content: Review standard details: Non-parallel lines = eventually cross in distance.

slides.title: Common Error: Tilted Intersections
slides.text: Another mistake is drawing intersecting lines that look perpendicular but are slightly tilted. We must verify they form a true ninety-degree angle using a corner. In math, we express this as: Tilted crossing = not perpendicular. . . . . . . How can you verify if two crossed lines are perpendicular?

examples.title: Example 23
examples.content: Review standard details: Tilted crossing = not perpendicular.

slides.title: Line Relationship Exercises
slides.text: Let's practice identifying lines. Look at different pairs of lines on your worksheet and classify them as parallel, intersecting, or perpendicular based on their crossings. In math, we express this as: Classify line pairs = parallel, perpendicular, intersecting. . . . . . What type of lines do the hands of a clock make at three?

examples.title: Example 24
examples.content: Review standard details: Classify line pairs = parallel, perpendicular, intersecting.

slides.title: Summary of Week 3 Lessons
slides.text: Let's review: we learned to identify and draw parallel lines, intersecting lines, and perpendicular lines, and to draw equal line segments using a metric ruler. In math, we express this as: Line types = parallel, perpendicular, and intersecting. . . . . . How do these line relationships help us draw shapes accurately?

examples.title: Example 25
examples.content: Review standard details: Line types = parallel, perpendicular, and intersecting.

challenge.q: [Advanced HOTS] Line A is parallel to Line B. Line B is parallel to Line C. What is the relationship between Line A and Line C?
options: They are parallel to each other

pages:
Exercise 1: Identifying Types of Lines

Write Parallel, Intersecting, or Perpendicular for each pair of lines below.

1. Lines that cross and form four square corners:
Perpendicular
2. Lines that run side-by-side and never cross:
Parallel

Exercise 2: Drawing Equal Line Segments

Answer the questions about ruler measurements and line segment lengths.

1. To draw a line segment of exactly 8 cm, where should you place the start point on the ruler?
0 mark

Exercise 3: Geometry in Art

Identify geometric line patterns found in local South Cotabato arts.

1. What type of line patterns are formed by the crossing warp and weft fibers in T'nalak weaving?
Intersecting

performance.title: [Advanced] Street Map Designer with Lines
performance.desc: Advanced Task: On a clean sheet of paper, design a simple road map for a barangay in Polomolok. Draw at least two parallel roads (label them Pineapple Road and Matutum Road). Draw another road that intersects both parallel roads (label it Mountain Road). Finally, draw a road that is perpendicular to Pineapple Road (label it Market Road). Draw two equal-sized houses using your ruler. Take a photo of your labeled map and upload it here.
labels: Correct parallel and perpendicular roads drawing
Correct drawing of two equal-sized houses using a ruler
